{{Distinguish|Endometritis}}
{{short description|Medical condition}}
{{good article}}
{{Use dmy dates|date=November 2025}}
{{Use American English|date=December 2025}}
{{cs1 config |name-list-style=vanc |display-authors=6}}
{{Infobox medical condition
| name = Endometriosis
| image = 202503 Endometriosis with annotation.svg
| alt = Diagram of uterus with endometriosis on Fallopian tubes and an endometrioma in the right ovary. Adhesions connect the right ovary to the uterus.
| caption = 
| field = [[Gynecology]]
| symptoms = [[Pelvic pain]], [[endometriosis and infertility|infertility]], [[painful periods]]
| complications = 
| onset = Adolescence or early 20s
| duration = Long-term
| causes = Unknown
| risks = Family history, early first period, short cycle length
| diagnosis = Based on symptoms, [[medical imaging]], [[tissue biopsy]]
| differential = [[Adenomyosis]], [[irritable bowel syndrome]], [[bladder pain syndrome]]
| prevention = 
| treatment = [[NSAID]]s, continuous birth control pills, [[intrauterine device with progestogen|hormonal IUD]] (coil), surgery
| medication = 
| prognosis = 
| frequency = Roughly 10% of women of reproductive age
}}

'''Endometriosis''' is a disease in which [[Tissue (biology)|tissue]] similar to the [[endometrium]]—the lining of the uterus (womb)—grows elsewhere in the body. The tissue most often grows close to the uterus, such as on the [[ovaries]], [[fallopian tube]]s, or the [[Peritoneum|lining of the pelvis]]. It can also appear on the [[Gastrointestinal tract|bowel]], [[bladder]], and in some cases on the [[lungs]] or skin.

Symptoms vary widely between individuals. Some have no symptoms, while for others it is a debilitating disease. Common symptoms include [[pelvic pain]], [[Heavy menstrual bleeding|heavy]] and [[Dysmenorrhea|painful periods]], pain with bowel movements, [[Dysuria|painful urination]], [[Dyspareunia|painful sex]], fatigue, and [[infertility]]. Beyond bodily symptoms, endometriosis can affect a person's [[mental health]] and social life. It is not known exactly what causes the disease. Possible causes include backward (retrograde) [[menstrual period]] flow, genetic factors, [[Hormone|hormones]], and problems with the [[immune system]].

A presumed diagnosis can be made based on symptoms and [[medical imaging]]; [[laparoscopy]] (keyhole surgery) with a [[biopsy]] can provide definite confirmation. Other causes of similar symptoms include [[adenomyosis]], [[Uterine fibroid|uterine fibroids]] (both conditions affecting the uterus), [[irritable bowel syndrome]], and [[bladder pain syndrome]]. On average, it takes 5–12 years from the start of symptoms to receive a diagnosis. Many women report being incorrectly told their symptoms are trivial or normal.

While there is no cure for endometriosis, several treatments may improve symptoms. These include [[pain medication]], hormonal treatments, or surgery. The recommended pain medication is usually a [[non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drug]] (NSAID), such as [[naproxen]]. Taking the [[birth control pill]] continuously or using a [[Hormonal intrauterine device|hormonal IUD]] (coil) are other first-line treatments. Alternative hormonal treatment can be tried if the pill or an IUD are not effective. Endometriosis may be removed surgically in women whose symptoms are not relieved by other treatments, or where endometriosis is associated with infertility.

Worldwide, endometriosis affects approximately 10% of the female population of reproductive age, representing about 190 million women. Symptoms typically start in [[adolescence]] or the early 20s, and tend to improve after [[menopause]] as estrogen levels go down. Most individuals benefit from drug treatment, even though this approach may lose effectiveness over time. Endometriosis can return after surgical removal.

==Subtypes==
{{multiple image
| perrow            = 2
| total_width       = 350
| image1            = Superficial peritoneal endometriosis.png
| alt1              = A small purple-black lesion among a peach-colored background
| image2            = Deep infiltrating endometriosis A.png
| alt2              = Larger red lesion in peach-colored background
| image3            = Perforierte Endometriosezyste.jpg
| alt3              = A silvery-brown cyst with a dark, viscous fluid leaking out
| image4            = Scar endometriosis after caesarian section.png
| alt4              = A small bump on the abdomen
| footer            = Subtypes of endometriosis. From top to bottom, left to right: superficial peritoneal endometriosis, deep infiltrating endometriosis, a ruptured chocolate cyst, and a mass of scar endometriosis encircled in red next to a [[caesarean section]] scar.
}}

Endometriosis occurs when [[endometrium]]-like tissue grows outside the uterus,<ref name=":7" /> forming patches called [[lesions]] or implants.<ref>{{Cite web |title=About Endometriosis |url=https://www.nichd.nih.gov/health/topics/endometri/conditioninfo |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20251219055230/https://www.nichd.nih.gov/health/topics/endometri/conditioninfo |archive-date=19 December 2025 |access-date=2026-03-25 |website=Eunice Kenny Shriver National Institute of Child Health and Human Development |language=en |url-status=live }}</ref> It is classified into four subtypes:<ref name=":7" />

; Superficial peritoneal endometriosis
: Small lesions that grow on the [[peritoneum]], the [[membrane]] lining the [[abdominal cavity]] and covering the surface of [[pelvis|pelvic]] organs
; Deep infiltrating endometriosis
: Lesions that grow into the tissue beneath the lining of the pelvis or into the muscle layers of pelvic organs like the [[bowel]] or [[bladder]]
; Ovarian endometriomas
: [[Cyst]]s that grow in the [[Ovary|ovaries]]
; Extrapelvic endometriosis
: Lesions outside the pelvic region, such as in the lungs or [[Thoracic diaphragm|diaphragm]]

Subtypes can exist in isolation or in combination with one another.<ref name=":7" /> Endometriomas appear dark brown, giving rise to the name "chocolate cysts", and are filled with old menstrual blood among other material.<ref name="Horne2022" /> When lesions grow more than 5&nbsp;mm beneath the peritoneal surface, they are classified as deep infiltrating endometriosis.<ref>{{cite journal |vauthors=Van den Bosch T, Van Schoubroeck D |date=2018 |title=Ultrasound diagnosis of endometriosis and adenomyosis: State of the art |url=https://lirias.kuleuven.be/handle/123456789/621035 |journal=Best Practice & Research. Clinical Obstetrics & Gynaecology |volume=51 |pages=16–24 |doi=10.1016/j.bpobgyn.2018.01.013 |pmid=29506961 |s2cid=3759091 |url-access=subscription}}</ref> Deep endometriosis can infiltrate the [[muscular layer]] of organs.<ref name=":7" /> It often appears as [[nodule (medicine)|nodule]]s, and can be associated with [[fibrosis]] and [[Adhesion (medicine)|adhesions]].<ref name=":7" />

Endometriosis most commonly affects the ovaries, the [[fallopian tube]]s between the ovaries and the uterus, the outer surface of the [[Uterus|uterus]], and the tissues that hold the uterus in place. Less common pelvic sites are the [[rectum]], bladder, bowel, [[vulva]], [[vagina]], and [[cervix]].<ref name="WH2025">{{cite web |date=24 October 2025 |title=Endometriosis |url=https://www.womenshealth.gov/a-z-topics/endometriosis |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250926224728/https://womenshealth.gov/a-z-topics/endometriosis |archive-date=26 September 2025 |access-date=31 October 2025 |publisher=Office on Women's Health}}</ref> Rarely, endometriosis appears outside the pelvis, such as on the lungs or [[Thoracic diaphragm|diaphragm]] ([[thoracic endometriosis]]), brain, or [[skin]].<ref name="WH2025" /><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Andres |first1=Marina P. |last2=Arcoverde |first2=Fernanda V. L. |last3=Souza |first3=Carolina C. C. |last4=Fernandes |first4=Luiz Flavio C. |last5=Abrão |first5=Mauricio Simões |last6=Kho |first6=Rosanne Marie |date=2020 |title=Extrapelvic Endometriosis: A Systematic Review |url=https://www.jmig.org/article/S1553-4650(19)31256-7/abstract |journal=Journal of Minimally Invasive Gynecology |language=English |volume=27 |issue=2 |pages=373–389 |doi=10.1016/j.jmig.2019.10.004 |issn=1553-4650 |pmid=31618674}}</ref> [[Cutaneous endometriosis|Scar endometriosis]] can form on the [[abdominal wall]] as a complication of [[surgery]], most often following a [[caesarean section]] or other pelvic surgery.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Gruber |first1=T. M. |last2=Lange |first2=K. |last3=Ebeling |first3=G. S. |last4=Henrich |first4=W. |last5=Mechsner |first5=S. |date=2025 |title=Scar endometriosis, a form of abdominal wall endometriosis–a neglected obstetrical complication? |journal=Archives of Gynecology and Obstetrics |volume=312 |issue=1 |pages=1–8 |doi=10.1007/s00404-024-07834-2 |issn=1432-0711 |pmc=12176990 |pmid=39607442}}</ref> It forms at or near the location of the surgical cut.{{sfn|ESHRE|2022|p=148}}

==Signs and symptoms==
Endometriosis can cause pain, fatigue, and infertility.<ref name=":15">{{Cite book|title=Gynecology Module Volume 3|last1=Sasamoto|first1=Naoko|year=2023|last2=Stacey|first2=Missmer|doi=10.3843/GLOWM.417643|issn=1756-2228|editor-last=Horne|editor-first=Andrew|editor-last2=Whitaker|editor-first2=Lucy|chapter=Endometriosis Symptoms and Signs|chapter-url=https://www.glowm.com/article/heading/vol-3--endometriosis--endometriosis-symptoms-and-signs/id/417643|series=The Continuous Textbook of Women's Medicine Series}}</ref> Some individuals have no symptoms, while others can experience severe, life-altering pain. The amount of pain is only weakly related to the anatomical extent of endometriosis. Those with limited endometriosis may have significant pain, while those with severe endometriosis may have few symptoms.<ref name="Horne2022" /> Symptoms typically start in adolescence or the early 20s and usually disappear during [[menopause]], as [[estrogen]] levels decline. For some, especially women using [[hormone replacement therapy]], symptoms may continue or even appear for the first time during menopause.<ref name=":7" />

Common symptoms include:<ref name=":15" /><ref>{{Cite web |title=What are the symptoms of endometriosis? |url=https://www.nichd.nih.gov/health/topics/endometri/conditioninfo/symptoms |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20251231103500/https://www.nichd.nih.gov/health/topics/endometri/conditioninfo/symptoms |archive-date=31 December 2025 |access-date=2026-03-07 |website=Eunice Kenny Shriver National Institute of Child Health and Human Development |language=en |url-status=live }}</ref>

* [[Dysmenorrhea|Painful periods]]
* Chronic pelvic pain
* [[Dyspareunia|Painful sex]]
* Painful urination or bowel movements
* Fatigue
* [[Heavy menstrual bleeding]]

Painful symptoms can occur anytime during the [[menstrual cycle]], but are usually more intense during menstruation; in other words, they follow a cyclical pattern.<ref name=":7" /> The pain has been described as dull and cramping in nature,<ref name=":12">{{Cite journal |last1=Oberegger |first1=Elisabeth |last2=Taxer |first2=Bernhard |date=2025 |title=Physiotherapeutische Differenzialdiagnose von Rückenschmerzen im Zusammenhang mit Endometriose |trans-title=Physiotherapeutic differential diagnosis of back pain associated with endometriosis |journal=Schmerz (Berlin, Germany) |volume=39 |issue=6 |pages=388–397 |doi=10.1007/s00482-024-00795-0 |issn=1432-2129 |pmc=12627120 |pmid=38393352}}</ref> and can interfere with daily activities, such as school, work, or participating in social events.<ref name=":15" /> Some individuals describe the sensation as resembling clawing fingernails or stabbing knives in the lower abdomen. The pain may radiate to the lower back.<ref name=":12" />

About a quarter of women diagnosed with endometriosis experience infertility.<ref name=":7" /> In some women, infertility is the only symptom they experience.{{Sfn|Solnik|Sanders|2025|p=11}} The [[World Health Organization]] estimates that endometriosis is the ultimate cause of female infertility in 4.8% of cases.{{Sfn|World Health Organization|2025|p=2}}

Endometriosis can also involve symptoms like [[constipation]], [[diarrhea]], [[nausea]], [[bloating]], and rectal or abdominal pain. This is sometimes caused by endometriosis on the bowels, but often due to concurrent [[irritable bowel syndrome]]. People with endometriosis often experience [[fatigue]], which is linked to [[insomnia]], [[Depression (mood)|depression]], and [[anxiety]].<ref name=":15" /> Deep infiltrating endometriosis in the bowel or bladder can cause [[bowel obstruction]] and cyclical [[blood in stool]] or [[Hematuria|blood in urine]].<ref name=":7" /><ref name=":15" />  

[[Thoracic endometriosis]]—endometriosis on the diaphragm, [[lung]]s, or [[pleura]] around the lungs—can cause cyclical cough and cyclical shoulder pain. When present in the lungs, it can cause cyclical [[hemoptysis|blood discharge from the lungs]] during menstruation.{{sfn|ESHRE|2022|p=149}} When found in the pleura, it can cause cyclical [[pneumothorax|collapsed lung]] and [[hemothorax|bleeding into the pleural space]]. Most often, the endometriosis is found in the right lung.{{sfn|ESHRE|2022|p=149}} Endometriosis around scars can be painful and swell during periods.<ref name="Horne2022" /> Sciatic endometriosis, which involves the [[sciatic nerve]], causes cyclical nerve pain in the leg.<ref name="Gandhi Wilson Liang Weissbart pp. 3–9">{{cite journal |vauthors=Gandhi J, Wilson AL, Liang R, Weissbart SJ, Khan SA|title=Sciatic endometriosis: A narrative review of an unusual neurogynecologic condition|journal=Journal of Endometriosis and Pelvic Pain Disorders|volume=13|issue=1|date=2020|issn=2284-0265|doi=10.1177/2284026520970813|pages=3–9|s2cid=228834273}}</ref>

===Concurrent conditions===
Endometriosis is associated with a higher risk of complications during pregnancy. Women with endometriosis have a three-fold increased risk of a [[Placenta praevia|placenta previa]], in which the [[placenta]] partially or completely covers the cervical opening. [[Preterm birth|Preterm delivery]] is almost 50% more likely. Other complications are [[stillbirth]], [[gestational hypertension]], [[pre-eclampsia]], and [[placental abruption]].<ref name=":15" /> The rare complication of [[spontaneous hemoperitoneum in pregnancy]], which is highly dangerous to both mother and fetus, is more common in women with endometriosis too.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Vercellini |first1=Paolo |last2=Viganò |first2=Paola |last3=Bandini |first3=Veronica |last4=Buggio |first4=Laura |last5=Berlanda |first5=Nicola |last6=Somigliana |first6=Edgardo |date=2023 |title=Association of endometriosis and adenomyosis with pregnancy and infertility |url=https://www.fertstert.org/article/S0015-0282(23)00227-3/fulltext |journal=Fertility and Sterility |language=English |volume=119 |issue=5 |pages=727–740 |doi=10.1016/j.fertnstert.2023.03.018 |issn=0015-0282 |pmid=36948440|doi-access=free }}</ref>

Beyond its effects in pregnancy, endometriosis is linked to [[cardiovascular disease]], particularly in those who have had the uterus and ovaries removed as treatment for endometriosis. Cohort studies have found associations with [[stroke]]s, heart attacks ([[myocardial infarction]]), [[high blood pressure]], and [[irregular heartbeat]].<ref name=":15" /> Depression and anxiety are more common in people with endometriosis compared to healthy people, but occur at the same rate as they do among people with other chronic pain conditions.{{Sfn|Solnik|Sanders|2025|p=12}} It is unclear how much this is caused by shared underlying mechanisms, the impact of severe symptoms, [[Social stigma|stigma]], diagnostic delays, or the ineffectiveness of treatment.<ref name=":15" />

Endometriosis often co-occurs with other pain disorders, [[Autoimmune disease|autoimmune disorders]] (such as [[lupus]] or [[Rheumatoid arthritis|arthritis]]), certain cancers, stroke, and [[long COVID]]. For instance, a quarter of women with endometriosis have at least one other pain condition.<ref name=":7" /> Women with endometriosis have an increased risk of developing ovarian and [[thyroid cancer]]s compared to women without the condition, as well as a slightly higher risk of [[breast cancer]].<ref name="Kvaskoff Mahamat-Saleh Farland Shigesi pp. 393–420">{{cite journal |vauthors=Kvaskoff M, Mahamat-Saleh Y, Farland LV, Shigesi N, Terry KL, Harris HR, Roman H, Becker CM, As-Sanie S, Zondervan KT, Horne AW, Missmer SA |date=2021 |title=Endometriosis and cancer: a systematic review and meta-analysis |journal=Human Reproduction Update |volume=27 |issue=2 |pages=393–420 |doi=10.1093/humupd/dmaa045 |hdl=20.500.11820/fa3c779d-3cc7-4d0d-b93a-d7176fd8244d |pmid=33202017 |hdl-access=free}}</ref>

==Risk factors==
===Genetics===
Inheritance is a significant but not the sole risk factor for endometriosis. Studies attribute 50% of the risk to genetics; the other 50% is attributed to environmental factors.<ref name="Montgomery2020">{{cite journal |vauthors=Montgomery GW, Mortlock S, Giudice LC |title=Should Genetics Now Be Considered the Pre-eminent Etiologic Factor in Endometriosis? |journal=[[Journal of Minimally Invasive Gynecology]] |volume=27 |issue=2 |pages=280–286 |date=2020 |pmid=31683028 |pmc=7863762 |doi=10.1016/j.jmig.2019.10.020}}</ref> Individuals who have a [[first-degree relative]] with endometriosis—such as a parent, sibling, or child—have a three- to nine-fold higher risk of developing the condition.<ref>{{Cite journal |date=2026 |title=Diagnosis of Endometriosis: ACOG Clinical Practice Guideline No. 11 |url=https://journals.lww.com/10.1097/AOG.0000000000006181 |journal=Obstetrics & Gynecology |language=en |volume=147 |issue=3 |pages=432–448 |doi=10.1097/AOG.0000000000006181 |issn=0029-7844|url-access=subscription }}</ref> At least 42 different [[Locus (genetics)|loci]] (regions on a [[chromosome]]) have been associated with endometriosis risk. The genes linked to endometriosis risk help control cancer-related processes, [[sex-hormone]] signals, womb development, molecules related to inflammation and adhesions, and the growth of new blood vessels.<ref name=":14">{{Cite journal |last1=Rahmioglu |first1=Nilufer |last2=Mortlock |first2=Sally |last3=Ghiasi |first3=Marzieh |last4=Møller |first4=Peter L. |last5=Stefansdottir |first5=Lilja |last6=Galarneau |first6=Geneviève |last7=Turman |first7=Constance |last8=Danning |first8=Rebecca |last9=Law |first9=Matthew H. |last10=Sapkota |first10=Yadav |last11=Christofidou |first11=Paraskevi |last12=Skarp |first12=Sini |last13=Giri |first13=Ayush |last14=Banasik |first14=Karina |last15=Krassowski |first15=Michal |date=2023 |title=The genetic basis of endometriosis and comorbidity with other pain and inflammatory conditions |journal=Nature Genetics |language=en |volume=55 |issue=3 |pages=423–436 |doi=10.1038/s41588-023-01323-z |pmid=36914876 |pmc=10042257 |issn=1546-1718}}</ref>

There is significant overlap between the genetic basis of endometriosis, other pain conditions, and inflammatory conditions. For instance, endometriosis shares some genetic risk factors with [[migraine]], headaches, neck, and [[back pain]]. Among inflammatory conditions, it shares genetic variation with [[asthma]] and [[osteoarthritis]].<ref name=":14" />

===Reproductive and environmental factors===
People whose menstrual outflow is obstructed are at risk of developing endometriosis. This could for instance be because of an [[imperforate hymen]], a birth defect where the vagina is completely blocked.{{sfn|Solnik|Sanders|2025|p=5}} Another type of obstruction occurs when [[Uterine septum|extra tissue divides]] the vagina into two sides. This can happen alongside a [[double uterus]]. If one side is blocked (an obstructed [[Hemivagina|hemi-vagina]]), menstrual blood cannot flow out and becomes trapped.<ref>{{Cite web |date=5 February 2025 |title=Obstructed hemi-vagina |url=https://www.uclh.nhs.uk/patients-and-visitors/patient-information-pages/obstructed-hemi-vagina |access-date=28 February 2026 |website=University College London Hospitals NHS Foundation Trust |language=en |archive-date=6 September 2025 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250906093622/https://www.uclh.nhs.uk/patients-and-visitors/patient-information-pages/obstructed-hemi-vagina |url-status=live }}</ref> Other risk factors are having a [[Menarche|first period]] before age 12, a menstrual cycle of fewer than 28 days, a low BMI, and [[Nulliparity|not having had children]].<ref name=":7" />

Little is known about environmental risk factors. Exposure to certain classes of environmental pollutants seems to raise risks. The most studied of these are [[Endocrine disruptor|endocrine disruptors]]—chemicals that interfere with hormones such as estrogen.<ref name=":13">{{Cite journal |last1=Coiplet |first1=Eléna |last2=Courbiere |first2=Blandine |last3=Agostini |first3=Aubert |last4=Boubli |first4=Léon |last5=Bretelle |first5=Florence |last6=Netter |first6=Antoine |date=2022 |title=Endometriosis and environmental factors: A critical review |url=https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2468784722001015 |journal=Journal of Gynecology Obstetrics and Human Reproduction |volume=51 |issue=7 |article-number=102418 |doi=10.1016/j.jogoh.2022.102418 |pmid=35667590 |issn=2468-7847|url-access=subscription }}</ref> They include [[Dioxins and dioxin-like compounds|dioxins]], [[phthalates]], [[bisphenol A]], and [[polychlorinated biphenyl]]. Epidemiological and experimental data suggest that exposure to some of them can increase the risk of endometriosis.<ref name=":13" /><ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Dutta |first1=Sudipta |last2=Banu |first2=Sakhila K. |last3=Arosh |first3=Joe A. |date=2023 |title=Endocrine disruptors and endometriosis |url=https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0890623822001691 |journal=Reproductive Toxicology |volume=115 |pages=56–73 |doi=10.1016/j.reprotox.2022.11.007 |pmid=36436816 |bibcode=2023RepTx.115...56D |issn=0890-6238|url-access=subscription }}</ref> [[Shift work|Night work]] and [[red meat]] consumption are associated with an increased risk too.<ref name=":13" />

==Mechanism==
[[File:Characteristics_of_endometriosis.webp|thumb|upright=1.3|Six characteristics of endometriosis: estrogen dependency, retrograde menstruation, [[angiogenesis]], local inflammation, [[oxidative stress]] and [[Stem cell|stem cells]]|alt=diagram showing the 'puzzle pieces' of endometriosis mechanisms]]
[[File:Endometriosis_cells_types_and_innvervation.jpg|upright=1.3|thumb|Endometriosis lesions have both endometrium-like [[stromal cell]]s (which normally forms supporting tissue<ref>{{cite journal|url=https://academic.oup.com/reproduction/article/160/3/R25/8235588|title=The ovarian stroma as a new frontier |doi=10.1530/REP-19-0501|vauthors=Kinnear HM, Tomaszewski CE, Chang AL, Moravek MB, Xu M, Padmanabhan V, Shikanov A|date=2020|volume=160|issue=3|journal=Reproduction}}</ref>) and [[epithelial cells]], and associated blood vessels and nerves.|alt=schematic showing uterus with endometriosis on the outside, and a close-up of a lesion. The closeup shows regions with epithelial cells surrounded by stromal cells, and associated blood supply and nerves.]]
Endometriosis is an [[inflammatory disease]] defined by the presence of tissue similar to the lining of the uterus (the [[endometrium]]) elsewhere in the body.<ref name=":7" /> The development and growth of these lesions requires [[estrogen]].{{sfn|ESHRE|2022|p=6}} The lesions promote local inflammation and immune system dysregulation. They also trigger the formation of [[Adhesion (medicine)|adhesions]] ([[fibrous]] bands that form between [[Tissue (biology)|tissues]] and [[Organ (biology)|organs]]) and [[fibrosis]] (excess connective tissue from healing, a form of [[Scar|scarring]]). It is not well understood how endometriosis causes infertility and pain.<ref name=":7" />

===Formation===
The main theories for the formation of the endometrium-like tissue outside the womb are backward flow of menstrual blood, [[metastasis]] via the [[Lymphatic system|lymphatic]] or the [[circulatory system]], and local transformation of peritoneal cells into endometrial-like cells (''coelomic metaplasia'').<ref name="zondervan32212520" />

During [[menstruation]], some menstrual blood, tissue, and fluid can flow backward through the fallopian tubes into the pelvic area (the [[peritoneal cavity]]). This backward flow (''retrograde menstruation'') is thought to be the main reason why endometriosis develops inside the pelvic cavity. However, this explanation alone is not enough, because almost all women have some backward flow of menstrual fluid, but only 10% develop endometriosis.<ref name="Horne2022">{{cite journal|author1-link=Andrew W. Horne|vauthors=Horne AW, Missmer SA|title=Pathophysiology, diagnosis, and management of endometriosis|journal=BMJ|volume=379|article-number=e070750|date=November 2022|pmid=36375827|doi=10.1136/bmj-2022-070750|hdl-access=free|hdl=20.500.11820/a2c07717-cf08-4119-b0f4-b7f8aa50193e}}</ref> Evidence supporting the theory comes from [[retrospective cohort study|retrospective epidemiological studies]] and DNA analysis.<ref name="Malvezzi Marengo Podgaec Piccinato p." /><ref>{{cite journal|last=Bulun|first=Serdar E.|date=2025|title=Endometriosis and ovulatory menstruation: beyond the Sampson principle|journal=The Journal of Clinical Investigation|volume=135|issue=13|article-number=e188787|doi=10.1172/JCI188787|issn=1558-8238|pmc=12208542|pmid=40590223}}</ref> Furthermore, only animals with a [[menstrual cycle]], such as [[Rhesus macaque|rhesus monkeys]] and [[baboon]]s, develop endometriosis. In contrast, animals like [[rodent]]s and [[non-human primates]] with an [[estrous cycle]], in which the endometrium is reabsorbed rather than shed, do not develop the disease naturally.<ref name="Malvezzi Marengo Podgaec Piccinato p.">{{cite journal|vauthors=Malvezzi H, Marengo EB, Podgaec S, Piccinato CA|title=Endometriosis: current challenges in modeling a multifactorial disease of unknown etiology|journal=Journal of Translational Medicine|volume=18|issue=1|article-number=311|date=August 2020|pmid=32787880|pmc=7425005|doi=10.1186/s12967-020-02471-0|publisher=Springer Science and Business Media LLC|doi-access=free|title-link=doi}}</ref>

Endometriosis has been documented in men, prepubescent girls, and in female fetuses, demonstrating that menstruation is not required for the condition to develop. One explanation for endometriosis in girls before puberty is the coelomic metaplasia theory, that certain cells in the [[peritoneum]] may undergo [[metaplasia]] (transformation) into endometrium-like tissue. Endometriosis in men may be explained by the persistence of [[Müllerian duct|Müllerian]] remnants; normally, these cells disintegrate during male embryonic development.<ref name='Pasalic2023'>{{cite journal|last1=Pašalić|first1=Elma|last2=Tambuwala|first2=Murtaza M.|last3=Hromić-Jahjefendić|first3=Altijana|date=2023|title=Endometriosis: Classification, pathophysiology, and treatment options|url=https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0344033823005484|journal=Pathology - Research and Practice|volume=251|doi=10.1016/j.prp.2023.154847|issn=0344-0338|pmid=37844487|url-access=subscription|article-number=154847}}</ref>

[[Stem cell|Stem cells]] may contribute to the formation of endometriosis.<ref name=":3">{{Cite journal |last1=Li |first1=Qiutong |last2=Shi |first2=Jinghua |last3=Yi |first3=Dai |last4=Li |first4=Xiaoyan |last5=Gu |first5=Zhiyue |last6=Yan |first6=Hailan |last7=Leng |first7=Jinhua |date=2024 |title=The pathogenesis of endometriosis and adenomyosis: insights from single-cell RNA sequencing |url=https://academic.oup.com/biolreprod/article/110/5/854/7612756 |journal=Biology of Reproduction |language=en |volume=110 |issue=5 |pages=854–865 |doi=10.1093/biolre/ioae032 |pmid=38386960 |issn=0006-3363|url-access=subscription }}</ref> Stems cells in the basal layer of the endometrium play a role in renewing the tissue after menstruation. In women with endometriosis, more tissue is shed from this layer during menstruation, allowing more stem cells to flow back into the peritoneum with retrograde menstruation, and form lesions.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Liu |first1=Yanli |last2=Zhang |first2=Zhiqin |last3=Yang |first3=Fen |last4=Wang |first4=Hongmei |last5=Liang |first5=Shengying |last6=Wang |first6=Huiling |last7=Yang |first7=Jun |last8=Lin |first8=Juntang |date=2020 |title=The role of endometrial stem cells in the pathogenesis of endometriosis and their application to its early diagnosis |url=https://academic.oup.com/biolreprod/article/102/6/1153/5712138 |journal=Biology of Reproduction |language=en |volume=102 |issue=6 |pages=1153–1159 |doi=10.1093/biolre/ioaa011 |pmid=31965165 |issn=0006-3363|url-access=subscription }}</ref> Stem cells from [[bone marrow]] may drive the further growth of lesions, but also provide an explanation for endometriosis outside the pelvic region.<ref name="Horne2022" /> An alternative explanation for extrapelvic endometriosis is [[metastasis]] via the [[Lymphatic system|lymphatic]] or the [[circulatory system]].<ref name=":7" />

Most women with retrograde menstruation do not develop endometriosis, so other factors must also contribute to its development.<ref name="Horne2022" /> For endometriosis to develop, its cells must evade destruction by the immune system, attach to a surface, and promote the formation of new blood vessels, similar to some cancerous [[Neoplasm|tumors]].<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Zhang |first1=Manlin |last2=Xu |first2=Tongtong |last3=Tong |first3=Deming |last4=Li |first4=Siman |last5=Yu |first5=Xiaodan |last6=Liu |first6=Boya |last7=Jiang |first7=Lili |last8=Liu |first8=Kuiran |date=2023 |title=Research advances in endometriosis-related signaling pathways: A review |journal=Biomedicine & Pharmacotherapy |volume=164 |article-number=114909 |doi=10.1016/j.biopha.2023.114909 |pmid=37210898 |issn=0753-3322|doi-access=free }}</ref> Immune dysfunction could be involved in the disease in various ways. It may lead to a decrease in destruction of endometrial cells outside the womb. Additionally, a local inflammatory environment may make it more likely that the cells attach to a surface, and may reduce programmed cell death ([[apoptosis]]).<ref name="Horne2022" /> [[Angiogenesis]], the formation of new [[Blood vessel|blood vessels]], is a likely driver in the formation and maintenance of endometriotic lesions.<ref name=":3" /><ref>{{Cite journal |last=Dudley |first=Andrew C. |last2=Griffioen |first2=Arjan W. |date=2023 |title=Pathological angiogenesis: mechanisms and therapeutic strategies |journal=Angiogenesis |volume=26 |issue=3 |pages=313–347 |doi=10.1007/s10456-023-09876-7 |issn=1573-7209 |pmc=10105163 |pmid=37060495}}</ref> Cells in the peritoneum of women with endometriosis release more [[growth factors]] that stimulate angiogenesis.<ref name="Horne2022" />

Endometriosis lesions require estrogen to grow.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Vannuccini |first=Silvia |last2=Clemenza |first2=Sara |last3=Rossi |first3=Margherita |last4=Petraglia |first4=Felice |date=2022 |title=Hormonal treatments for endometriosis: The endocrine background |journal=Reviews in Endocrine and Metabolic Disorders |language=en |volume=23 |issue=3 |pages=333–355 |doi=10.1007/s11154-021-09666-w |issn=1573-2606 |pmc=9156507 |pmid=34405378}}</ref><ref name="zondervan32212520" /> This estrogen is produced both within the lesions themselves and in other parts of the body.<ref name="zondervan32212520" /> Sometimes, the cells in endometriosis are [[progesterone]]-resistant or develop resistance during treatment with the hormone. This weakened response to progesterone allows endometriosis to continue growing outside the womb.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Tang |first1=Hsiao-Chien |last2=Lin |first2=Ting-Chien |last3=Wu |first3=Meng-Hsing |last4=Tsai |first4=Shaw-Jenq |date=2024 |title=Progesterone resistance in endometriosis: A pathophysiological perspective and potential treatment alternatives |journal=Reproductive Medicine and Biology |volume=23 |issue=1 |article-number=e12588 |doi=10.1002/rmb2.12588 |issn=1445-5781 |pmc=11157498 |pmid=38854774}}</ref>

===Pain===
There are multiple possible causes of [[pain]] associated with endometriosis and the main drivers of pain may differ from person to person. The growth of [[Neuroangiogenesis|new blood vessels and nerves]] in lesions is thought to play an important role.<ref name="Horne2022" /> The inflammation and fibrosis around endometriosis can cause pain by activating pain-sensing [[Nerve|nerves]].<ref name=":9">{{Cite journal |last1=Coxon |first1=Lydia |last2=Demetriou |first2=Lysia |last3=Vincent |first3=Katy |date=2024 |title=Current developments in endometriosis-associated pain |journal=Cell Reports Medicine |volume=5 |issue=10 |article-number=101769 |doi=10.1016/j.xcrm.2024.101769 |pmid=39413731 |issn=2666-3791|pmc=11513828 }}</ref> [[Neuropathic pain]] can arise from damage to nerves. In rare cases, endometriosis can infiltrate or compress nerves,<ref name=":7" /> and damage to nerves might also occur due to surgery.<ref name=":9" /> Estrogens can increase communication between immune cells and nerves in lesions, which may contribute to further pain.<ref name="Horne2022" /> Finally, there may be [[Systemic inflammation|systemic (body-wide) inflammation]], involving [[white blood cells]]. This can lead to [[nociplastic pain]], which amplifies pain signals and reduces pain inhibition. This type of pain is associated with poor sleep, memory problems, and fatigue.<ref name=":7" />

===Infertility===
{{Main|Endometriosis and infertility}}

The infertility associated with endometriosis likely has multiple causes. Inflammation and hormonal dysfunction explain some instances: they can disrupt the ovarian environment and reduce egg quality. The [[ovarian reserve]], the number of viable egg cells in the ovaries, is typically lower in those with endometriosis. In particular, endometriomas may reduce ovarian reserve in affected ovaries.<ref name="Horne2022" /> There is contradictory evidence on whether endometriosis causes reduced [[ovulation]]. Anatomical distortions, for instance from adhesions, can explain further instances of infertility, and in severe cases, sperm or egg cells may be fully blocked. Pain during sex may lead couples to avoid it, leading to fewer opportunities for [[natural conception]].<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Elizur |first1=Shai E. |last2=Mostafa |first2=Jollanar |last3=Berkowitz |first3=Elad |last4=Orvieto |first4=Raoul |date=2025 |title=Endometriosis and infertility: pathophysiology, treatment strategies, and reproductive outcomes |journal=Archives of Gynecology and Obstetrics |language=en |volume=312 |issue=4 |pages=1037–1048 |doi=10.1007/s00404-025-08124-1 |issn=1432-0711 |pmc=12414060 |pmid=40689964}}</ref>

==Diagnosis==
A health history and a [[physical examination]] may lead a health care practitioner to suspect endometriosis. A presumed diagnosis can be made based on symptoms together with [[ultrasound]] or [[MRI imaging]]. The gold standard for definite [[diagnosis]] is via surgery and a [[biopsy]], but there has been a shift away from requiring surgical confirmation to prevent treatment delays.<ref name=":7" /> 

Diagnosis takes an average of five to twelve years from the onset of symptoms.<ref name=":7" /> This delay has remained persistent. On average, individuals wait one to four years before seeking medical help, which might be explained by the normalization of symptoms, the lack of awareness, and lack of [[access to healthcare]]. After seeking help, it can take up to nine years to get a diagnosis, reflecting a normalization of symptoms by healthcare providers, limited expertise and tools, and general inefficiencies in healthcare.<ref>{{Cite journal|title=Time to Diagnose Endometriosis: Current Status, Challenges and Regional Characteristics—A Systematic Literature Review|journal=BJOG: An International Journal of Obstetrics & Gynaecology|date=2025|issn=1471-0528|pmc=11625652|pmid=39373298|pages=118–130|volume=132|issue=2|doi=10.1111/1471-0528.17973|language=en|first1=Pauline|last1=De Corte|first2=Moritz|last2=Klinghardt|first3=Sophia|last3=von Stockum|first4=Klaas|last4=Heinemann}}</ref>

===Physical examination===
Initial assessment includes screening of risk factors and an evaluation of symptoms. Both general symptoms and those linked to deep endometriosis or endometriosis outside the pelvis are assessed. A trauma-informed approach validates pain, fosters trust, and screens for trauma before a physical exam.<ref name=":7" />

The physical examination can include an [[Abdominal examination|abdominal exam]], a single digit exam of the vagina and pelvic floor, a [[bimanual exam]] and examination with a [[Speculum (medicine)|speculum]]. Findings that suggest endometriosis include nodularity or tenderness at the back of the uterus, decreased mobility of the uterus, and the finding of tender blue-gray nodules in the vagina. Even if nothing is found on examination, endometriosis may still be present.<ref name=":7" />

===Ultrasound===
[[File:Endometrioma.jpg|thumb|upright=1.2|[[Transvaginal ultrasonography|Transvaginal ultrasound]] of a 67 x 40 mm [[endometrioma]], exhibiting a characteristic ground-glass [[Echogenicity|appearance on ultrasound]]<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Keckstein |first1=J. |last2=Hoopmann |first2=M. |last3=Merz |first3=E. |last4=Grab |first4=D. |last5=Weichert |first5=J. |last6=Helmy-Bader |first6=S. |last7=Wölfler |first7=M. |last8=Bajka |first8=M. |last9=Mechsner |first9=S. |last10=Schäfer |first10=S. |last11=Krentel |first11=H. |last12=Hudelist |first12=G. |date=2023 |title=Expert opinion on the use of transvaginal sonography for presurgical staging and classification of endometriosis |journal=Archives of Gynecology and Obstetrics |language=en |volume=307 |issue=1 |pages=5–19 |doi=10.1007/s00404-022-06766-z |issn=1432-0711 |pmc=9837004 |pmid=36367580}}</ref>|alt=see caption]]
[[Vaginal ultrasonography|Vaginal ultrasound]] can be used to diagnose endometriomas and deep infiltrating endometriosis. It cannot rule out a diagnosis of endometriosis; a normal scan may require further investigations.{{sfn|Solnik|Sanders|2025|p=93-94}} Vaginal ultrasound is inexpensive, easily accessible, has no [[Contraindication|contraindications]], and requires no preparation.<ref name=":0">{{cite web |date=4 May 2018 |title=Endometriosis – Diagnosis, treatment and patient experiences |url=https://www.sbu.se/en/publications/sbu-assesses/endometriosis--diagnosis-treatment-and-patient-experiences/ |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210119024015/https://www.sbu.se/en/publications/sbu-assesses/endometriosis--diagnosis-treatment-and-patient-experiences/ |archive-date=19 January 2021 |access-date=13 June 2018 |publisher=[[Swedish Agency for Health Technology Assessment and Assessment of Social Services]] (SBU)|last1=Berterö|first1=Carina|last2=Edelstam|first2=Greta|last3=Kjer|first3=Jens Jørgen|last4=Melin|first4=Anna-Sofia|last5=Rhodin|first5=Annica|last6=Valentin|first6=Lil}}</ref> The ultrasound can be extended to look at the front and back of the pelvis. This allows the examiner to assess deep infiltrating endometriosis and organ mobility.<ref name=":2">{{cite journal |vauthors=Fang J, Piessens S|title=A step-by-step guide to sonographic evaluation of deep infiltrating endometriosis|journal=Sonography|date=2018|volume=5|issue=2|pages=67–75|doi=10.1002/sono.12149|doi-access=|url=https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/sono.12149|url-access=subscription}}</ref>

If a transvaginal ultrasound is not suitable or declined, an alternative is an [[Abdominal ultrasonography|ultrasound via the lower abdomen]].{{sfn|Solnik|Sanders|2025|p=93}} Abdominal ultrasounds can find endometriomas, but are not suitable for assessing deep endometriosis.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Smith |first1=Anne K. |last2=Bhullar |first2=Simran |last3=Vash-Margita |first3=Alla |date=2026 |title=Endometriosis in adolescents: A state-of-the-art review |url=https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1521693426000064 |journal=Best Practice & Research Clinical Obstetrics & Gynaecology |volume=105 |article-number=102706 |doi=10.1016/j.bpobgyn.2026.102706 |issn=1521-6934|url-access=subscription }}</ref>

===MRI===
[[File:Trichromatic_Color_MRI_of_ovarian_cysts.jpg|thumb|upright=1.2|Trichromatic color MRI of ovarian cysts. The bottom right cyst is endometriosis.|alt=Four MRI images of ovarian cysts. The MRI image distinguishes fat, water, blood, and blood products and therefore shows the endometrioma in a unique colour.]]
[[Magnetic resonance imaging|MRI]] is another means of detecting lesions without surgery. MRI is not widely used due to its cost and limited availability.<ref name="Imaging">{{cite journal |vauthors=Nisenblat V, Bossuyt PM, Farquhar C, Johnson N, Hull ML |date=2016 |title=Imaging modalities for the non-invasive diagnosis of endometriosis |journal=Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews |volume=2016 |issue=2 |doi=10.1002/14651858.cd009591.pub2 |pmc=7100540 |pmid=26919512 |article-number=CD009591}}</ref> It can reliably detect endometriomas and deep infiltrating endometriosis. It is sometimes used for planning surgery, for instance if an ultrasound is unclear, or for diagnosis if a transvaginal ultrasound is not appropriate or is declined. The [[field of view]] is larger in an MRI compared to an ultrasound, which allows a larger part of the bowel to be assessed, as well as extra-pelvic endometriosis.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Quesada |first1=Juan |last2=Härmä |first2=Kirsi |last3=Reid |first3=Shannon |last4=Rao |first4=Tanushree |last5=Lo |first5=Glen |last6=Yang |first6=Natalie |last7=Karia |first7=Sonal |last8=Lee |first8=Emmeline |last9=Borok |first9=Nira |date=2023 |title=Endometriosis: A multimodal imaging review |url=https://www.ejradiology.com/article/S0720-048X(22)00460-0/fulltext |journal=European Journal of Radiology |volume=158 |article-number=110610 |doi=10.1016/j.ejrad.2022.110610 |issn=0720-048X |pmid=36502625}}</ref>

===Keyhole surgery===
Laparoscopy ([[Laparoscopy|keyhole surgery]]) is a surgical procedure where a camera is used to look inside the [[abdominal cavity]]. Laparoscopy with a biopsy is the most accurate way to diagnose endometriosis.<ref name=":7" /> It can be used when endometriosis is suspected, and imaging does not show any clear causes for symptoms.<ref name="Horne2022" /> In nearly 40% of women undergoing laparoscopic surgery for pelvic pain, no cause is discovered.<ref name="Horne2022" />

Surgery is not always needed: if imaging does not show clear signs of endometriosis, clinicians may first try treatment and give a presumed diagnosis if symptoms improve ('[[empirical therapy|empirical treatment]]').{{sfn|ESHRE|2022|p=30}} Surgery for diagnosis allows for surgical treatment of uncomplicated endometriosis at the same time.<ref name="Horne2022" />

The lesions of superficial endometriosis often appear dark blue or black. In the earlier stages of disease, they may be white, red, or yellow-brown. Ovarian cysts (also called chocolate cysts) are typically dark brown. [[Adhesion (medicine)|Adhesions]] are made up of fibrous scar tissue. Deep endometriosis looks like multiple distinct nodules.<ref name="Horne2022" /> A biopsy may not detect endometriosis even when the disease is present, particularly in younger women. As such, a biopsy alone cannot be used to rule out a diagnosis of endometriosis.<ref name="Horne2022" /> For confirmation, biopsy samples should show at least two of the following features:<ref>{{cite web | vauthors = Han L, Garcia R, Busca A, Parra-Herran C | date = November 2023 | veditors = Turashvili G, Skala SL | url = https://www.pathologyoutlines.com/topic/ovarynontumorendometriosis.html | title = Endometriosis | website = Pathology Outlines | access-date = 18 March 2020 | archive-date = 7 August 2020 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20200807014147/http://www.pathologyoutlines.com/topic/ovarynontumorendometriosis.html | url-status = live }}</ref>
* Endometrial type [[stroma (animal tissue)|stroma]]
* Endometrial [[epithelium]] with glands
* Evidence of chronic bleeding, such as [[hemosiderin]] (iron) deposits

<gallery mode="packed" heights="175">
File:Endometriosis, abdominal wall.jpg|Endometriosis, abdominal wall. The yellow [[Adipose tissue|adipose (fat) tissue]] contains dark blood-filled cysts, white fibrous bands, and areas of bleeding.|alt=yellow-white abdominal wall with dark spots
File:Endometriosis of the ovary.jpg|[[Micrograph|Microscope image]] showing endometriosis (right) and ovarian stroma (left)|alt=Purple microscope image showing two types of tissue
File:Endometrioma1.jpg|Microscope image of the wall of an endometrioma. All features of endometriosis are present (endometrial [[gland]]s, endometrial [[stroma (animal tissue)|stroma]] and iron in [[macrophage]]s)
</gallery>

===Staging===
There are various staging or classification systems commonly used. Endometriosis is most often staged with the revised [[American Society of Reproductive Medicine]] (rASRM) staging system.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Alves |first1=João Sequeira |last2=Meneses |first2=Tânia |last3=Mariano |first3=Mafalda |last4=Serra |first4=Sofia Silvério |last5=Costa |first5=Teresa |last6=Martins |first6=João Paulo |last7=Rabishong |first7=Benoit |last8=Lima |first8=Jorge |date=2025 |title=Is endometriosis Staging Related to the Type and Intensity of Patients' Complaints? A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis |url=https://linkinghub.elsevier.com/retrieve/pii/S1553465025009562 |journal=Journal of Minimally Invasive Gynecology |language=en |doi=10.1016/j.jmig.2025.11.014|url-access=subscription }}</ref> rASRM classifies endometriosis as stage I–IV, ranging from minimal (stage I) to severe (stage IV).<ref name="Crump2024">{{cite journal |last1=Crump |first1=Jessica |last2=Suker |first2=Adriana |last3=White |first3=Louise |date=2024 |title=Endometriosis: A review of recent evidence and guidelines |url=https://www1.racgp.org.au/ajgp/2024/january-february/endometriosis |journal=Australian Journal of General Practice |volume=53 |issue=1–2 |pages=11–18 |doi=10.31128/AJGP/04-23-6805 |pmid=38316472 |doi-access=free}}</ref> These stages correlate poorly with how much pain women have.<ref name="Horne2022" /> The scale uses a point system that assesses lesions and adhesions during surgery. The ENZIAN system is more detailed and provides a structured description of endometriosis by anatomical compartments, particularly for deep endometriosis, including the extent of lesions within each compartment.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Keckstein |first1=Jörg |last2=Hudelist |first2=Gernot |date=2021 |title=Classification of deep endometriosis (DE) including bowel endometriosis: From r-ASRM to #Enzian-classification |url=https://linkinghub.elsevier.com/retrieve/pii/S1521693420301759 |journal=Best Practice & Research Clinical Obstetrics & Gynaecology |language=en |volume=71 |pages=27–37 |doi=10.1016/j.bpobgyn.2020.11.004 |url-access=subscription |archive-date=21 May 2025 |access-date=7 February 2026 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20250521185834/https://linkinghub.elsevier.com/retrieve/pii/S1521693420301759 |url-status=live }}</ref> The ENZIAN system does not predict well which symptoms women have.<ref name="Horne2022" />

The [[American Association of Gynecologic Laparoscopists]] (AAGL) endometriosis staging system, introduced in 2021, was designed to correlate well with the complexity of surgery. Like rASRM, it divides endometriosis into four stages.<ref name="Pasalic2023" /> [[Fertility]] is assessed separately with the Endometriosis Fertility Index (EFI).<ref name="Horne2022" /> The EFI is scored from 0 to 10, with higher scores indicating a greater likelihood of natural pregnancy.<ref name="Pasalic2023" />

===Differential diagnosis===
Various conditions exhibit similar symptoms to endometriosis. [[Adenomyosis]], the growth of endometrium-like tissue in the muscles of the uterus, is one example. It sometimes shows characteristics on an MRI that distinguish it from endometriosis. Bladder pain syndrome ([[interstitial cystitis]]) is mainly characterized by painful urination, but also causes more diffuse pain. It can be distinguished from endometriosis with a [[cystoscopy]] (bladder examination), which shows pinpoint [[Hemorrhage|bleeding spots]] in the bladder in bladder pain syndrome. [[Irritable bowel syndrome]] can sometimes be distinguished from endometriosis via the presence of diarrhea or constipation, and can be diagnosed in the absence of endometriosis via imaging or surgery.{{sfn|Solnik|Sanders|2025|p=21}} Other conditions with overlapping symptoms are [[Uterine fibroid|uterine fibroids]], [[Stenosis of uterine cervix|cervical stenosis]], and pelvic floor [[Myofascial pain syndrome|myofascial pain]].<ref name=":7" />

==Prevention==
According to the World Health Organization, there is no known way to prevent endometriosis.<ref name="WHO Fact Sheet">{{cite web|title=Endometriosis|url=https://www.who.int/news-room/fact-sheets/detail/endometriosis|access-date=8 February 2026|publisher=[[World Health Organization]] (WHO)|date=15 October 2025|archive-date=4 December 2023|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231204160817/https://www.who.int/news-room/fact-sheets/detail/endometriosis|url-status=live}}</ref> There are associations between some modifiable risk factors and endometriosis: women with endometriosis tend to consume more red meat, [[Trans fat|trans fats]], [[Alcoholic beverage|alcohol]], and [[caffeine]]. Physical activity does not seem to prevent endometriosis, but can lessen pain. It is unclear whether these factors cause the disease. Whether [[hormonal contraception]] can prevent endometriosis is controversial.{{Sfn|ESHRE|2022|p=158}}

==Management==
While there is no cure for endometriosis, there are treatments for pain and endometriosis-associated infertility. Pain can be treated with hormones, painkillers, or, in severe cases, surgery.<ref name=":23">{{cite web |date=11 December 2025 |title=What are the treatments for endometriosis? |url=https://www.nichd.nih.gov/health/topics/endometri/conditioninfo/treatment |access-date=7 February 2026 |publisher=Eunice Kennedy Shriver National Institute of Child Health and Human Development (NICHD) |archive-date=6 December 2019 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20191206051701/https://www.nichd.nih.gov/health/topics/endometri/conditioninfo/treatment |url-status=live }}</ref> The goal of management is to provide pain relief, to restrict the progression of the process, and to restore or preserve fertility where needed.<ref name="zondervan32212520" /> The UK [[National Institute for Health and Care Excellence]] recommends starting initial medication for those with suspected endometriosis at the same time as referral for investigations such as an ultrasound.{{sfn|NICE|2024|pp=11-12}} Alternatively, initial treatment is started after an ultrasound.<ref name=":7" /> Surgery is an option when medical treatment is insufficiently effective, for advanced disease or infertility.{{sfn|Solnik|Sanders|2025|p=26}}

===Hormonal medications===
[[File:Mirena IntraUterine System.jpg|thumb|The hormonal coil (Mirena) is one of the treatment options for endometriosis.|alt=a hormonal coil placed in the womb.]]

The various hormonal options to treat endometriosis have comparable efficacy. Individuals respond differently to the medications, finding the best option often involves trial and error to balance pain relief with minimal [[Side effect|side effects]]. Hormonal treatment is not suitable for women who are trying to become pregnant.<ref name=":7" />

[[Progestin]]-only hormonal suppression (progestogen) is a first-line therapy. It comes in different forms and includes the [[Hormonal intrauterine device|hormonal coil]] (an intrauterine device), the oral drug [[dienogest]], an injection of [[medroxyprogesterone acetate]] every three months or an [[subdermal implant|implant under the skin]].<ref name=":7" /> Dienogest, which may be more effective than injections,<ref name=":10">{{cite journal |last1=Chen |first1=Innie |last2=Kives |first2=Sari |last3=Zakhari |first3=Andrew |last4=Nguyen |first4=Dong Bach |last5=Goldberg |first5=Hanna R |last6=Choudhry |first6=Abdul J |last7=Le |first7=Ai-Lien |last8=Kowalczewski |first8=Emilie |last9=Schroll |first9=Jeppe Bennekou |date=2025 |title=Progestagens for pain symptoms associated with endometriosis |journal=Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews |volume=2025 |issue=10 |article-number=CD002122 |doi=10.1002/14651858.CD002122.pub3 |pmid=41065045 |pmc=12509269 |pmc-embargo-date=October 9, 2026 }}</ref> is not available on its own in the US.<ref name=":7" /> Oral progestins likely reduce overall pain and period pain compared to placebo, and may also help with pelvic pain. It is unclear how well they work compared to other hormonal therapies.<ref name=":10" />

[[Combined oral contraceptive pill|Combined estrogen-progestin birth control pills]] are another first-line treatment. They usually cause few side effects and the recommendation is to use the pills continuously to stop periods. A formulation with the lowest level of estrogen that can still stop periods is advised. The combined pill may not be suitable for those with liver disease, [[migraine with aura]], or those at high risk of [[Thromboembolism|blood clots]].<ref name=":7">{{cite journal |last1=As-Sanie |first1=Sawsan |last2=Mackenzie |first2=Scott C. |last3=Morrison |first3=Leigh |last4=Schrepf |first4=Andrew |last5=Zondervan |first5=Krina T. |last6=Horne |first6=Andrew W. |last7=Missmer |first7=Stacey A. |date=2025 |title=Endometriosis |url=https://ora.ox.ac.uk/objects/uuid:b471a0f8-ff5a-4d13-b435-b3ea5e3c2b3c/files/s0z709017z |journal=JAMA  |volume=334 |issue=1 |pages=64–78 |doi=10.1001/jama.2025.2975 |pmid=40323608 |url-access= |doi-access=}}</ref> 

[[Gonadotropin-releasing hormone modulator|Gonadotropin-releasing hormone (GnRH) modulator]]s are second-line treatments. These drugs include [[GnRH agonist]]s such as [[leuprorelin]] and [[GnRH antagonist]]s such as [[elagolix]], which decrease estrogen levels.<ref name=":7" /> GnRH agonists mimic the effects of menopause, and seem more effective than placebo or oral progestin at reducing pain.<ref name=":11">{{cite journal |last1=Veth |first1=Veerle B |last2=van de Kar |first2=Majorie MA |last3=Duffy |first3=James MN |last4=van Wely |first4=Madelon |last5=Mijatovic |first5=Velja |last6=Maas |first6=Jacques WM |date=2023 |editor-last=Cochrane Gynaecology and Fertility Group |title=Gonadotropin-releasing hormone analogues for endometriosis |journal=Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews |volume=2023 |issue=6 |article-number=CD014788 |doi=10.1002/14651858.CD014788.pub2 |pmid=37341141 |pmc=10283345 }}</ref> They come with side effects of [[hot flash]]es and [[decreased bone density]]. GnRHs can be prescribed with hormonal 'add-back' therapy or with calcium-regulating agents to reduce the amount of bone loss.<ref name=":7" /><ref name=":11" />

[[Aromatase inhibitor]]s, such as [[anastrozole]] and [[letrozole]], are third-line treatments and block estrogen production throughout the body. Common side effects are hot flashes, [[night sweats]], and [[Ovarian cyst#Functional|functional cysts]].<ref name=":7" /><ref name="Garzon Laganà Barra Casarin 2020 pp. 1377–1388">{{cite journal |vauthors=Garzon S, Laganà AS, Barra F, Casarin J, Cromi A, Raffaelli R, Uccella S, Franchi M, Ghezzi F, Ferrero S |date=2020 |title=Aromatase inhibitors for the treatment of endometriosis: a systematic review about efficacy, safety and early clinical development |journal=Expert Opinion on Investigational Drugs |volume=29 |issue=12 |pages=1377–1388 |doi=10.1080/13543784.2020.1842356 |pmid=33096011 |s2cid=225058751}}</ref> In premenopausal women, these should be taken with other hormones (such as the combined pill) to prevent ovarian stimulation and menopause symptoms. They can be an option for post-menopausal women who still have endometriosis symptoms, as their action is not limited to suppressing estrogen from the ovaries. Evidence is limited.<ref name=":7" />

===Pain medication===
NSAIDs like [[naproxen]] or [[ibuprofen]] are anti-inflammatory medications commonly used for endometriosis pain.<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Brown J, Crawford TJ, Allen C, Hopewell S, Prentice A | title = Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs for pain in women with endometriosis | journal = Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews | volume = 1 | issue = 1 | article-number = CD004753 | date = January 2017 | pmid = 28114727 | pmc = 6464974 | doi = 10.1002/14651858.CD004753.pub4 }}</ref> They are first-line treatments and can work well for mild pain.<ref name=":7" /><ref name=":23" /> NSAIDs can be used separately (for instance, if fertility is a priority), or in combination with hormonal treatment.<ref name=":7" /> Evidence for their effectiveness is limited, with only one small randomized trial conducted. NSAIDs can have side effects, predominantly gastrointestinal, but they are generally safe to try.{{sfn|ESHRE|2022|pp=35-36}} NSAIDs pose some risks for people with heart disease, kidney disease, or uncontrolled [[hypertension]].<ref name=":7" /> 

===Surgery===
{{See also|Endometrioma#Surgery}}
[[File:Blausen_0602_Laparoscopy_02.png|thumb|upright=1.2|[[Laparoscopy]] of the uterus and fallopian tubes. The abdomen is filled with gas so that the surgeon can see better and have space for instruments.<ref>{{cite journal |vauthors=Yu T, Cheng Y, Wang X, Tu B, Cheng N, Gong J, Bai L |title=Gases for establishing pneumoperitoneum during laparoscopic abdominal surgery |journal=Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews |year=2017 |volume=2017 |issue=6 |article-number=CD009569 |doi=10.1002/14651858.CD009569.pub3 |pmid=28635028
 |pmc=6481852}}</ref>|alt=a small instrument is inserted around the belly button and shines a light on the womb and fallopian tubes]]
Clinical guidelines recommend surgery when medical treatment does not work sufficiently, has unacceptable side effects, or should be avoided due to health risks (a [[contraindication]]). Large endometriomas can only effectively be treated with surgery. Surgery is also recommended when deep endometriosis causes problems in the bowels or [[urinary tract]], such as obstruction. It is unclear what the effect of surgery is for pain relief in cases of superficial peritoneal endometriosis.<ref name=":7" />

Laparoscopy (keyhole surgery) is the standard surgical approach. Treatment consists of the removal of endometriosis lesions and the restoration of pelvic anatomy via the division of [[adhesions]].{{sfn|ESHRE|2022|p=48}} The removal takes place via [[Surgery#Types_of_surgery|excision]] (cutting out) or [[electrosurgery]] (coagulation or ablation/vaporization).{{sfn|ESHRE|2022|pp=48, 51}}{{sfn|Solnik|Sanders|2025|p=27}} With laparoscopic surgery, small instruments are inserted through incisions to remove the endometriosis tissue and adhesions. After surgery, people can usually return home the same day.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.gloshospitals.nhs.uk/your-visit/patient-information-leaflets/laparoscopic-keyhole-surgery-endometriosis-ghpi0796_02_19/|date=2024|access-date=21 November 2025|publisher=NHS Gloucestershire Hospitals|title=Laparoscopic (keyhole) surgery for endometriosis}}</ref>

Two literature reviews have compared excision to ablation. A 2017 literature review found that excision improved some outcomes compared to ablation for endometriosis in general. A 2021 literature review on minimal to mild endometriosis found no difference. For deep endometriosis, excision is the standard therapy, as ablation does not allow the surgeon to see if all endometriosis is removed.{{sfn|ESHRE|2022|p=50}} In the United States, some specialists trained in excision for endometriosis do not accept health insurance because insurance companies do not reimburse the higher costs of this procedure over ablation.<ref>{{cite news |date=16 July 2023 |title=Endometriosis, a painful and often overlooked disease, gets attention in a new film |url=https://www.npr.org/sections/health-shots/2023/07/16/1186533247/endometriosis-a-painful-and-often-overlooked-disease-gets-attention-in-a-new-fil |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230717173453/https://www.npr.org/sections/health-shots/2023/07/16/1186533247/endometriosis-a-painful-and-often-overlooked-disease-gets-attention-in-a-new-fil |archive-date=17 July 2023 |access-date=17 July 2023 |publisher=[[NPR]] |vauthors=Muraskin A}}</ref>

Endometriomas are usually excised (removed completely). Compared to drainage and coagulation of the cyst, excision makes it less likely the cysts and pain symptoms come back. However, excision may damage fertility, as it can affect the [[ovarian reserve]], the amount of [[Egg cell|egg cells]] that can be fertilized.<ref name="Horne2022" /><ref name=":7" />

For deep endometriosis, surgery improves quality of life and pain symptoms.{{Sfn|ESHRE|2022|p=55}} However, the procedure can be complicated, especially if the lesions are in or near the bowel, [[ureter]] (which transports urine from the [[kidney]]s to the [[bladder]]), or the chest, and requires an interdisciplinary surgical team in those cases. For instance, for [[rectovaginal]] endometriosis, a study reported 7% of surgeries had complications.<ref name=":7" /> When endometriosis involves the bowel or bladder, part of the affected organ may be removed.{{sfn|ESHRE|2022|pp=54-55}}<ref name="NHS">{{cite web|url=https://www.nhs.uk/conditions/endometriosis/|publisher=NHS|title=Endometriosis|access-date=21 November 2025|date=27 August 2024|archive-date=18 November 2025|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20251118180813/https://www.nhs.uk/conditions/endometriosis/|url-status=live}}</ref>

For women who still have significant pain after hormonal treatment and other surgery, and do not want to become pregnant, a [[hysterectomy]] (removal of the uterus) can be offered. This is done in combination with removal of endometriosis lesions. Removal of the uterus may be beneficial if the uterus itself is affected by [[adenomyosis]]. When the ovaries are removed too, women will experience early menopause and may need [[hormone replacement therapy]]. Removal of the ovaries comes with cardiovascular, metabolic, and mental health risks.{{sfn|ESHRE|2022|pp=60-61}}<ref name=":7" />

====Recurrence and postoperative hormonal suppression====
In an analysis with a median follow-up of 24 months, pain after surgery recurred in about 16% of women.<ref name=":7" /> Endometriosis recurrence following surgery is estimated as 21.5% at 2 years and 40–50% at 5 years. Hormonal therapy before surgery has little effect on recurrence, but treatment afterwards reduces the risk.<ref name="Horne2022" /> At a median follow-up of 18 months (studies varied between 12 and 36 months),<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Zakhari |first=Andrew |last2=Delpero |first2=Emily |last3=McKeown |first3=Sandra |last4=Tomlinson |first4=George |last5=Bougie |first5=Olga |last6=Murji |first6=Ally |date=2021 |title=Endometriosis recurrence following post-operative hormonal suppression: a systematic review and meta-analysis |url=https://academic.oup.com/humupd/article/27/1/96/5918116 |journal=Human Reproduction Update |language=en |volume=27 |issue=1 |pages=96–107 |doi=10.1093/humupd/dmaa033 |issn=1355-4786 |pmc=7781224 |pmid=33020832}}</ref> endometriosis recurred in 26% of women without postoperative hormonal suppression, compared with 10% of women who received it.<ref name=":7" /> The risk of recurrence is higher in younger women and in those with a less aggressive surgery.{{sfn|Solnik|Sanders|2025|p=27}}

===Additional therapies===
There is limited knowledge about the effectiveness of additional or [[adjunct therapy|adjunct therapies]] for endometriosis. Additional therapies sometimes used are [[pelvic floor physical therapy]], [[exercise]], [[acupuncture]], [[pain education]], or [[psychotherapy]] targeting pain.<ref name=":7" />  Diets high in [[antioxidants]] might help reduce period pain, based on a small number of studies.<ref name=":7" /><ref>{{cite journal | vauthors=Meneghetti JK, Pedrotti MT, Coimbra IM, ((Cunha-Filho JSL)) | journal=Reproductive Sciences | title=Effect of Dietary Interventions on Endometriosis: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis of Randomized Controlled Trials | volume=31 | issue=12 | pages=3613–3623| date=2024 | doi=10.1007/s43032-024-01701-w | pmid=39358652 }}</ref>

===Infertility===
{{Main|Endometriosis and infertility}}

[[File:InVitroFertilization.jpg|thumb|Assistive reproductive technology like IVF can help with fertility in endometriosis.]]
Infertility can be treated with [[assistive reproductive technology]] (ART), such as [[In vitro fertilisation|in vitro fertilization]] (IVF), or with surgery.{{sfn|Solnik|Sanders|2025|pp=28-29}} IVF procedures are effective in improving fertility in many women with endometriosis. IVF is increasingly recommended over surgery for older women or for those where there might be multiple reasons why they struggle to conceive.{{sfn|Solnik|Sanders|2025|p=29}} It does not increase recurrence of endometriosis.{{sfn|ESHRE|2022|p=118}} The Endometriosis Fertility Index can help guide decisions on treatment of infertility.{{sfn|ESHRE|2022|p=12}} Surgery is typically not recommended before starting ART.{{sfn|ESHRE|2022|p=13}}

Surgery to remove endometriomas can help with fertility. They can be cut out (in a cystectomy) or drained and destroyed (ablation). The ablation technique may better preserve the number of remaining viable eggs (the ovarian reserve), compared to cutting out the endometrioma.{{sfn|NICE|2024|pp=18-19}} On the other hand, cutting out the endometrioma may help more with pain.{{sfn|Solnik|Sanders|2025|p=29}} Surgery likely also helps with infertility in the case of superficial peritoneal endometriosis.<ref name=":7" /> Receiving hormonal suppression therapy after surgery might help with endometriosis recurrence and pregnancy,<ref>{{cite journal | vauthors = Chen I, Veth VB, Choudhry AJ, Murji A, Zakhari A, Black AY, Agarpao C, Maas JW | title = Pre- and postsurgical medical therapy for endometriosis surgery | journal = Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews | volume = 11 | issue = 12 | article-number = CD003678 | date = 2020 | pmid = 33206374 | pmc = 8127059 | doi = 10.1002/14651858.CD003678.pub3 }}</ref> but evidence for pregnancy outcomes is mixed.{{sfn|NICE|2024|pp=19, 28}} Both the UK [[National Institute for Health and Care Excellence]] (NICE) and the [[European Society of Human Reproduction and Embryology]] recommend against hormonal suppression to improve fertility.{{sfn|NICE|2024|pp=19, 28}}{{sfn|ESHRE|2022|p=83}}

==Prognosis==
Endometriosis is often a long-term condition, with symptoms typically emerging during adolescence and easing after menopause. For some women, pain persists after menopause. Treatments, whether medical or surgical, can alleviate symptoms but do not provide a definitive cure.<ref name=":17">{{Cite web|url=https://cks.nice.org.uk/topics/endometriosis/background-information/prognosis/|title=Endometriosis: what is the prognosis?|date=July 2025|website=NICE Clinical Knowledge Summaries (CKS)|last=NICE}}</ref> The disease does not always worsen; in studies that tracked people over time, endometriosis became worse in 29%, improved in 42%, and stayed the same in 29% of cases.<ref name="Horne2022" /> 

Around 70% of people see initial improvements with medical therapy, but the drugs can lose their effectiveness over time.<ref name=":6">{{Cite journal |last1=Giudice |first1=Linda C. |last2=Oskotsky |first2=Tomiko T. |last3=Falako |first3=Simileoluwa |last4=Opoku-Anane |first4=Jessica |last5=Sirota |first5=Marina |date=2023 |title=Endometriosis in the era of precision medicine and impact on sexual and reproductive health across the lifespan and in diverse populations |journal=The FASEB Journal |language=en |volume=37 |issue=9 |article-number=e23130 |doi=10.1096/fj.202300907 |doi-access=free |issn=0892-6638 |pmc=10503213 |pmid=37641572 }}</ref> The likelihood of symptoms returning after surgery is highly variable; studies have reported recurrence rates anywhere between 6% and 67%. For some, endometriosis becomes associated with persistent, complex pain, possibly linked to changes in the nervous system, as part of a constellation of [[Pain disorder|chronic pain disorders]].<ref name=":17" />

==Epidemiology==
Endometriosis is commonly reported to affect approximately 10% of women of reproductive age.<ref name=":7" /> Worldwide, this corresponds to an estimated 190 million girls and women.<ref name=":16">{{Cite web|url=https://cks.nice.org.uk/topics/endometriosis/background-information/prevalence/|title=Endometriosis: how common is it?|date=July 2025|website=NICE Clinical Knowledge Summaries (CKS)|last=NICE}}</ref> Around 22 million had a surgically confirmed diagnosis as of 2021.<ref name=":4">{{cite journal |last1=Li|first1=Ruijie|last2=Zhang|first2=Ling|last3=Liu|first3=Yi|date=2025|title=Global and regional trends in the burden of surgically confirmed endometriosis from 1990 to 2021|journal=Reproductive Biology and Endocrinology|volume=23|issue=1|page=88|doi=10.1186/s12958-025-01421-z|doi-access=free|issn=1477-7827|pmc=12144762|pmid=40483411}}</ref> It is difficult to determine an exact [[prevalence]], given the large delays in diagnosis and the need for a surgical confirmation for a definite diagnosis.<ref name=":16" /> The prevalence depends on the population studied and the way endometriosis is diagnosed (imaging, surgery). Of women with pelvic pain undergoing laparoscopy, 28% are diagnosed with endometriosis. Of those with infertility undergoing laparoscopy, 25% are diagnosed.<ref name=":7" />

The disease is most diagnosed when women are in their 30s, but symptoms typically start in their early 20s or in adolescence.<ref name=":7" /> Individuals can develop endometriosis symptoms before their [[Menarche|first period]] and in menopause too.<ref name=":16" /> Up to 4% of women still have endometriosis after reaching menopause, mostly in the form of ovarian endometriosis.<ref> {{Cite journal |last=Cassani |first=Chiara |last2=Tedeschi |first2=Sara |last3=Cucinella |first3=Laura |last4=Morteo |first4=Vittoria |last5=Camnasio |first5=Cristina Angela |last6=Tiranini |first6=Lara |last7=Martini |first7=Ellis |last8=Nappi |first8=Rossella E. |date=2024 |title=Menopause and endometriosis |url=https://linkinghub.elsevier.com/retrieve/pii/S037851222400224X |journal=Maturitas |volume=190 |pages= |doi=10.1016/j.maturitas.2024.108129 |issn=0378-5122|article-number=108129}} </ref> In the United States, the condition is more commonly diagnosed in white women than in black women.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Katon |first=Jodie G. |last2=Plowden |first2=Torie C. |last3=Marsh |first3=Erica E. |date=2023 |title=Racial disparities in uterine fibroids and endometriosis: a systematic review and application of social, structural, and political context |url=https://www.fertstert.org/article/S0015-0282(23)00060-2/fulltext |journal=Fertility and Sterility |language=English |volume=119 |issue=3 |pages=355–363 |doi=10.1016/j.fertnstert.2023.01.022 |issn=0015-0282 |pmid=36682686}}</ref> This may reflect [[Race and health|health inequities]] rather than true differences in prevalence.<ref name=":6" />

==History==
[[File:Trotula (BM 1896,0511.244).jpg|alt=Lithograph portrait of Trotula, facing front, wearing a veil and fur armbands|thumb|[[Trota of Salerno]], an early medieval physician, suggested ground goat [[testicle]]s as a treatment for ''uterine suffocation'', a concept that is consistent with endometriosis.{{sfn|Nezhat|Nezhat|Nezhat|2012|pp=2, 13}}]]

The earliest references to what may have been endometriosis are from [[Ancient Egypt]], nearly 4,000 years ago.{{sfn|Nezhat|Nezhat|Nezhat|2012|p=2}} In [[Ancient Greece]], the [[Hippocratic Corpus]]—written between the 5th and 4th centuries BCE—outlines symptoms similar to endometriosis, including adhesions and infertility.{{sfn|Nezhat|Nezhat|Nezhat|2012|p=3}} Over the centuries, numerous explanations for pelvic pain in women were proposed, often attributing the condition to supposed flaws in women themselves, such as madness, [[promiscuity]], or inherent weakness. Many women who received the now-discredited diagnosis of [[hysteria]] may have instead suffered from endometriosis.{{sfn|Nezhat|Nezhat|Nezhat|2012|pp=1, 56}}

[[File:John A. Sampson.jpg|thumb|[[John A. Sampson]], who defined endometriosis|alt=Black-and-white portrait of Sampson]]

Endometriosis proper was first defined between the end of the 19th century and the start of the 20th. Some attribute the first description to [[Carl von Rokitansky]] in 1860, who found endometrial glands in tissues using microscopy.{{sfn|Nezhat|Nezhat|Nezhat|2012|p=36}} Other authors consider it more likely he was describing [[Malignancy|malignant]] tissue, given his use of the term ''[[sarcoma]]'', which already then referred to cancer.<ref name=":19">{{cite journal|title=Who identified endometriosis?|url=https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0015028210009805|journal=Fertility and Sterility|date=2011|issn=0015-0282|pages=13–16|volume=95|issue=1|doi=10.1016/j.fertnstert.2010.06.027|first1=Giuseppe|last1=Benagiano|first2=Ivo|last2=Brosens|pmid=20673889|url-access=subscription|archive-date=13 April 2024|access-date=1 December 2025|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240413223916/https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0015028210009805|url-status=live}}</ref> Around 1896, Thomas Cullen and others described endometriosis and [[adenomyosis]] under the single name "adenomyoma". Between 1903 and 1920, Cullen showed that the tissue in adenomyomas was endometrial.<ref>{{citation|chapter=A History of Endometriosis|title=Endometriosis and Adenomyosis: Global Perspectives Across the Lifespan|date=2022|publisher=Springer Nature|place=Cham|isbn=978-3-030-97236-3|pages=3–13|doi=10.1007/978-3-030-97236-3_1|language=en|first1=Donatella|last1=Lippi|first2=Marwan|last2=Habiba|first3=Giuseppe|last3=Benagiano|editor-first=Engin|editor-last=Oral}}</ref> [[John A. Sampson]] defined endometriosis in 1932. He studied its [[pathogenesis]] and was the first to propose retrograde menstruation as a cause. He also suggested that endometrial tissue could be inadvertently transplanted during surgery, such as a [[Caesarean section]]. During operations, he observed that endometriotic lesions would bleed in sync with a woman's menstrual cycle.<ref name=":19" /> 

One early recommendation to prevent and treat endometriosis was pregnancy. For older women, another approach was surgery, involving [[oophorectomy]] (removal of the ovaries) and hysterectomy (removal of the uterus).<ref name=":20">{{citation|last1=Brosens|first1=Ivo|chapter=History of Endometriosis: A 20th-Century Disease|date=2011|title=Endometriosis|pages=1–18|editor-last=Giudice|editor-first=Linda C.|url=https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/9781444398519.ch1|access-date=2025-12-06|edition=1|publisher=Wiley|language=en|doi=10.1002/9781444398519.ch1|isbn=978-1-4443-3213-1|last2=Benagiano|first2=Giuseppe|editor2-last=Evers|editor2-first=Johannes L. H.|editor3-last=Healy|editor3-first=David L.|url-access=subscription}}</ref> In the 1940s, the only available hormonal therapies for endometriosis were high-dose [[testosterone (medication)|testosterone]] and [[high-dose estrogen]] therapy.<ref name="barbieri1992">{{cite journal|vauthors=Barbieri RL|title= Hormonal therapy of endometriosis|journal=Infertility and Reproductive Medicine Clinics of North America|volume=3|issue=1|pages=187–200|date=January 1992|quote=The hormonal therapy of endometriosis continues to evolve. In the 1940s and 1950s, high-dose testosterone and diethylstilbestrol regimens were the only hormonal agents available in the treatment of endometriosis. These agents, although efficacious, were associated with intolerable side effects. The current armamentarium of hormonal GnRH analogues, danazol, and synthetic progestins is efficacious and has fewer side effects.}}</ref> Success of high-dose estrogen therapy with [[diethylstilbestrol]] for endometriosis was first reported by Karnaky in 1948, but was associated with severe risks upon withdrawal.<ref name=":20" />  

[[Pseudopregnancy]] (high-dose estrogen–progestogen therapy simulating pregnancy) for endometriosis was first described by Kistner and Andrews in the late 1950s and became widely employed.<ref name=":20" /> [[Danazol]] was first described for endometriosis in 1971.<ref>{{cite journal|last1=Brosens|first1=Ivo|last2=Benagiano|first2=Giuseppe|date=2011|title=Endometriosis, a modern syndrome|journal=The Indian Journal of Medical Research|volume=133|issue=6|pages=581–593|issn=0971-5916|pmc=3135985|pmid=21727656}}</ref> It was used for some 40 years, but had [[Virilization|masculinizing]] side effects, including weight gain, [[Hirsutism|excessive body hair growth]], and [[Breast atrophy|shrinking of breasts]].<ref>{{cite journal|last1=Kalaitzopoulos|first1=Dimitrios Rafail|last2=Samartzis|first2=Nicolas
|last3=Kolovos|first3=Georgios N.|last4=Mareti|first4=Evangelia|last5=Samartzis|first5=Eleftherios Pierre|last6=Eberhard
|first6=Markus|last7=Dinas|first7=Kostantinos|last8=Daniilidis|first8=Angelos|date=2021|title=Treatment of endometriosis: a review with comparison of 8 guidelines|journal=BMC Women's Health|language=en|volume=21|issue=1|page=397|doi=10.1186/s12905-021-01545-5
|doi-access=free|issn=1472-6874|pmc=8628449|pmid=34844587}}</ref> From the 1990s, [[GnRH agonist]]s gained prominence for the treatment of endometriosis, but had disadvantages. They needed to be administered either nasally or via injections and were slow to act.<ref name=":18">{{cite journal|last=Surrey|first=Eric S.|date=2023|title=GnRH agonists in the treatment of symptomatic endometriosis: a review|journal=F&S Reports|series=Gonadotropin Releasing Hormone: 50 years of Innovation|volume=4|issue=2, Supplement|pages=40–45|doi=10.1016/j.xfre.2022.11.009|pmid=37223763
|pmc=10201290|issn=2666-3341}}</ref> Oral fast-acting [[Gonadotropin-releasing hormone antagonist|GnRH antagonists]], such as [[elagolix]], were introduced for endometriosis in 2018.<ref name=":18" /><ref name="pmid30763525">{{cite journal|vauthors=Barra F, Grandi G, Tantari M, Scala C, Facchinetti F, Ferrero S|title=A comprehensive review of hormonal and biological therapies for endometriosis: latest developments|journal=Expert Opin Biol Ther|volume=19|issue=4| pages=343–360|date=April 2019|pmid=30763525|doi=10.1080/14712598.2019.1581761|s2cid=73455399|hdl=11380/1201437|hdl-access=free}}</ref>

==Society and culture==
===Stigma and medical culture===
