Endometriosis is affected by multiple forms of stigma [[Infertility and childlessness stigmas|related to fertility]], pain, and menstruation, contributing to dismissal, delayed diagnosis, and adverse mental health outcomes''.''<ref name=":22" /> [[Menstrual stigma]] contributes by discouraging discussion among peers and by casting doubt on accounts of period pain.<ref name=":22">{{cite journal |vauthors=Kocas HD, Rubin LR, Lobel M|date=2023|title=Stigma and mental health in endometriosis|journal=European Journal of Obstetrics & Gynecology and Reproductive Biology|volume=19|doi=10.1016/j.eurox.2023.100228|pmc=10465859|pmid=37654520|doi-access=free|article-number=100228}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal|title=Endometriosis and stigmatization: A literature review|journal=Journal of Endometriosis and Pelvic Pain Disorders|date=2024|issn=2284-0265|pages=117–122|volume=16|issue=2|doi=10.1177/22840265241248488|language=en|first=Panagiota|last=Tragantzopoulou|doi-access=free}}</ref> There can also be stigma around painful sex.<ref name=":1" /> Cultural norms can reinforce these barriers. In India, unmarried women are often not offered a transvaginal ultrasound, because of taboos about sex before marriage. This causes delays in diagnosis.<ref>{{Cite news |last=Dehghan |first=Saeed Kamali |date=2023-05-01 |title='Hell on earth': India's taboos around women's pain leave endometriosis sufferers in agony |url=https://www.theguardian.com/global-development/2023/may/01/indias-taboos-around-womens-pain-leave-endometriosis-sufferers-in-agony-acc |access-date=2025-12-21 |work=The Guardian |language=en-GB |issn=0261-3077}}</ref> In other countries, women who have unperforated [[Hymen|hymens]] or are not sexually active may also be denied transvaginal ultrasounds.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Kim |first1=Doyoun |last2=Deslandes |first2=Alison |last3=Childs |first3=Jessie |last4=Osborne |first4=Brooke |date=2025 |title=Are Transvaginal Ultrasounds Withheld From Non-Sexually Active Adults? An Online Survey of Current Practices and Opinions of Australasian Sonographers |journal=Sonography |language=en |volume=12 |issue=3 |pages=332–344 |doi=10.1002/sono.12521 |issn=2202-8323|doi-access=free }}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |last=Galer |first=Sophia Smith |date=2022-12-13 |title=Women in the UK Are Being Denied Medical Treatment Because They're Virgins |url=https://www.vice.com/en/article/women-virgins-uk-ultrasounds/ |access-date=2026-03-06 |website=VICE |language=en-US |archive-date=7 March 2026 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20260307004903/https://www.vice.com/en/article/women-virgins-uk-ultrasounds/ |url-status=live }}</ref>

[[Race and ethnicity]] can impact how endometriosis affects one's life. For instance, the [[stereotype]] in the United States that non-white women have a higher pain threshold than white women may reduce quality of care.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Bougie |first1=Olga |last2=Nwosu |first2=Ikunna |last3=Warshafsky |first3=Chelsie |date=2022 |title=Revisiting the impact of race/ethnicity in endometriosis  |journal=Reproduction & Fertility |volume=3 |issue=2 |pages=R34–R41 |doi=10.1530/RAF-21-0106 |issn=2633-8386 |pmc=9066945 |pmid=35514542}}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal |last=Hoffman |first=Kelly M. |last2=Trawalter |first2=Sophie |last3=Axt |first3=Jordan R. |last4=Oliver |first4=M. Norman |date=2016 |title=Racial bias in pain assessment and treatment recommendations, and false beliefs about biological differences between blacks and whites |url=https://pnas.org/doi/full/10.1073/pnas.1516047113 |journal=Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences |language=en |volume=113 |issue=16 |pages=4296–4301 |doi=10.1073/pnas.1516047113 |issn=0027-8424 |pmc=4843483 |pmid=27044069}}</ref> Cultural attitudes also play a role; in a 2020 Puerto Rican study, stigma led to endometriosis pain being dismissed as {{Lang|es|changaría}} or {{Lang|es|changas}} by friends and family, local terms for whining or complaining, often directed at children.<ref name=":8">{{cite journal |vauthors=Matías-González Y, Sánchez-Galarza AN, Flores-Caldera I, Rivera-Segarra E |date=2021 |title="Es que tú eres una changa": stigma experiences among Latina women living with endometriosis |journal=Journal of Psychosomatic Obstetrics and Gynaecology |volume=42 |issue=1 |pages=67–74 |doi=10.1080/0167482X.2020.1822807 |pmc=8893272 |pmid=32964770 |s2cid=221862356}}</ref> 

Further barriers come from outdated standards for laparoscopic evaluation and lack of understanding of the disease.<ref name=":1">{{cite journal |vauthors=As-Sanie S, Black R, Giudice LC, Gray Valbrun T, Gupta J, Jones B, Laufer MR, Milspaw AT, Missmer SA, Norman A, Taylor RN, Wallace K, Williams Z, Yong PJ, Nebel RA |date=2019 |title=Assessing research gaps and unmet needs in endometriosis |journal=American Journal of Obstetrics and Gynecology |volume=221 |issue=2 |pages=86–94 |doi=10.1016/j.ajog.2019.02.033 |pmid=30790565 |s2cid=73480251}}</ref> Healthcare providers sometimes dismiss described symptoms as normal menstruation, or [[wikt:psychologize|psychologize]] the issue (ascribing the pain to a psychological cause).<ref name=":22" /> Menstrual disorders are generally not covered extensively in medical education, leading to gaps in knowledge of healthcare professionals.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Harris |first1=Sarah |last2=Tata |first2=Laila J. |last3=Qureshi |first3=Nadeem |last4=Read |first4=Katie |last5=Bains |first5=Manpreet |date=2025 |title=The Experiences of Endometriosis Patients During Primary Healthcare Encounters: A Systematic Review of Qualitative Evidence |journal=Women's Reproductive Health |pages=1–14 |doi=10.1080/23293691.2025.2545308 |issn=2329-3691}}</ref>

===Economic burden===
The economic burden of endometriosis is substantial.<ref name=":21">{{Cite journal |last1=Darbà |first1=Josep |last2=Marsà |first2=Alicia |date=2022 |title=Economic Implications of Endometriosis: A Review |journal=PharmacoEconomics |language=en |volume=40 |issue=12 |pages=1143–1158 |doi=10.1007/s40273-022-01211-0 |pmid=36344867 |issn=1179-2027}}</ref> Endometriosis has direct and indirect costs, which include loss of work days, direct costs of treatment, symptom management, and treatment of other associated conditions such as depression or chronic pain.<ref name=":5">{{cite journal |vauthors=Gao X, Outley J, Botteman M, Spalding J, Simon JA, Pashos CL |date=2006 |title=Economic burden of endometriosis |journal=Fertility and Sterility |volume=86 |issue=6 |pages=1561–72 |doi=10.1016/j.fertnstert.2006.06.015 |pmid=17056043 |s2cid=20623034 |doi-access=free |title-link=doi}}</ref> Surgery is the main component of medical costs. Costs vary greatly between countries. Annual [[productivity]] losses ranged from $293 per person in Nigeria to $33,428 in Italy (in 2022 currency values), while annual direct medical costs ranged from $1459 to $20,239 per woman.<ref name=":21" /> Some women are unable to work due to endometriosis: a survey of Puerto Rican women showed a 20% absence from work,<ref>{{cite journal |vauthors=Soliman AM, Coyne KS, Gries KS, Castelli-Haley J, Snabes MC, Surrey ES |date=2017 |title=The Effect of Endometriosis Symptoms on Absenteeism and Presenteeism in the Workplace and at Home |journal=Journal of Managed Care & Specialty Pharmacy |volume=23 |issue=7 |pages=745–754 |doi=10.18553/jmcp.2017.23.7.745 |pmc=10398072 |pmid=28650252 |doi-access=free |title-link=doi}}</ref> whereas a survey of Swedish women with endometriosis found that 32% did not work.<ref>{{cite journal |vauthors=Grundström H, Hammar Spagnoli G, Lövqvist L, Olovsson M |date=2020 |title=Healthcare Consumption and Cost Estimates Concerning Swedish Women with Endometriosis |journal=Gynecologic and Obstetric Investigation |volume=85 |issue=3 |pages=237–244 |doi=10.1159/000507326 |pmid=32248191 |s2cid=214811610 |doi-access=free |title-link=doi}}</ref>

===Research funding===
Like many conditions mainly affecting women, endometriosis is underfunded in relation to its [[disease burden]].<ref>{{Cite journal|title=Women's health research lacks funding – these charts show how|url=https://www.nature.com/immersive/d41586-023-01475-2/index.html|access-date=2025-12-21|website=Nature|language=en|date=2023|last=Smith|first=Kerry|volume=617}}</ref> For example, in the US, the NIH spent 5 to 10 times less on endometriosis research between 2015 and 2019 than on the average condition, relative to the number of [[Disability-adjusted life year|healthy life years lost]] to each disease.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Mirin |first=Arthur A. |date=2021 |title=Gender Disparity in the Funding of Diseases by the U.S. National Institutes of Health |journal=Journal of Women's Health |volume=30 |issue=7 |pages=956–963 |doi=10.1089/jwh.2020.8682 |issn=1540-9996 |pmc=8290307 |pmid=33232627}}</ref> In the EU, endometriosis got significantly less funding than 9 comparator conditions, including [[psoriasis]] and [[male infertility]].<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Viganò |first1=Paola |last2=Casalechi |first2=Maíra |last3=Dolmans |first3=Marie-Madeleine |date=2024 |title=European Union underinvestment in endometriosis research |journal=Journal of Endometriosis and Uterine Disorders |volume=5 |article-number=100058 |doi=10.1016/j.jeud.2023.100058 |issn=2949-8384|doi-access=free }}</ref>

==Research directions==
A priority area of research is the search for endometriosis [[biomarker]]s (for example, something detectable via a blood test), which can help with earlier diagnosis.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Pant |first1=Anuja |last2=Moar |first2=Kareena |last3=K. Arora |first3=Taruna |last4=Maurya |first4=Pawan Kumar |date=2023 |title=Biomarkers of endometriosis |url=https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0009898123003650 |journal=Clinica Chimica Acta |volume=549 |article-number=117563 |doi=10.1016/j.cca.2023.117563 |pmid=37739024 |issn=0009-8981|url-access=subscription }}</ref> Studies have examined potential biomarkers such as [[microRNA]]s, [[glycoprotein]]s, and immune markers in blood, menstrual, and urine samples, but none have shown the high accuracy needed for clinical use yet. [[CA-125]], a tumor marker, has been studied extensively. It is elevated in endometriosis, but also in many other conditions, and so cannot be used on its own. MicroRNAs might be most promising, but levels are not consistent among studies, making them a challenging target.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Agostinis |first1=C. |last2=Battista |first2=N. |last3=Costanzo |first3=M. |year=2021 |title=The Search for Biomarkers in Endometriosis: a Long and Windy Road |journal=Reproductive Sciences |volume=28 |issue=10 |pages=2751–2766 |doi=10.1007/s43032-021-00668-2 |pmid=34159571 }}</ref>

Medical management of endometriosis is typically based on hormonal therapy, but these treatments can produce undesirable side effects, driving the search for alternatives. Emerging strategies target endometriosis as an inflammatory, metabolic, or pain disorder. Anti-inflammatory research targets include [[anakinra]], a drug used in [[rheumatoid arthritis]]. The cancer drug [[dichloroacetic acid]] is being explored for its potential metabolic effects in endometriosis. Pain-focused treatments under investigation include [[Cannabinoid|cannabinoid extracts]], migraine medications, and therapies directed at affected nerves.<ref name="Saunders Horne 2021 pp. 2807–2824">{{cite journal |vauthors=Saunders PT, Horne AW|title=Endometriosis: Etiology, pathobiology, and therapeutic prospects|journal=Cell|volume=184|issue=11|pages=2807–2824|date=2021|pmid=34048704|doi=10.1016/j.cell.2021.04.041|hdl=20.500.11820/bb7ded31-cc3d-449e-a0dc-ce4b1a0531d2|s2cid=235226513|doi-access=free|title-link=doi|hdl-access=free}}</ref>

Little research has been done on the differences between lesions (the [[Molecular phenotyping|molecular phenotypes]]). Classifying lesions might make it possible to design more targeted therapies, an approach that has worked well for cancer.<ref name=":24" /> Endometriosis has been studied in a large variety of [[Model organism|animal models]] (e.g. mice developed to have endometriosis<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Zeng |first=Yan |last2=Hang |first2=Fu |last3=Peng |first3=Chuyu |last4=Zhao |first4=Ling |last5=Ou |first5=Shanshan |last6=Luo |first6=Liumei |last7=Liu |first7=Bo |date=2024 |title=Research progress in rodent models of endometriosis |url=https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0165037824000287 |journal=Journal of Reproductive Immunology |volume=163 |article-number=104219 |doi=10.1016/j.jri.2024.104219 |issn=0165-0378}}</ref>), but the tissue can differ substantially from human endometriosis.<ref name=":24" /> One alternative is the use of [[Organoid|organoids]] with cells derived from women with endometriosis. These miniature organs can then be studied ''[[in vitro]]'' to investigate the disease mechanism more precisely.<ref name=":24">{{Cite journal |last=Kyei-Barffour |first=Isaac |last2=Montgomery |first2=Grant W. |last3=McKinnon |first3=Brett D. |date=2026 |title=Variation in endometriosis aetiology, potential impact on lesion heterogeneity and the consequences for research and treatment |url=https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949838426000022 |journal=Journal of Endometriosis and Uterine Disorders |volume=14 |article-number=100152 |doi=10.1016/j.jeud.2026.100152 |issn=2949-8384}}</ref>
